title: The part that rehearses conversations obsessively before having them
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You've been rehearsing for hours. The conversation hasn't happened yet.

You're lying in bed at 2 AM. Or you're driving to work. Or you're staring at the shower wall. And your brain is running lines. You're playing out every possible version of a conversation you haven't even had yet. You're preparing for their objections, their anger, their disappointment, their silence. You're crafting the perfect response to something they haven't even said. And then you're rewriting it. And then you're imagining their face when you say it. And then you're imagining the worst possible version of that face.

I know this part. I've been this part. I still am this part, sometimes.

Here's the thing. That part of you that rehearses conversations obsessively before having them? It's not crazy. It's not broken. It's not a sign that you're "too anxious" or "too controlling" or "too much." That part is a protector. And it's been doing its job for a very long time.

What this part is actually trying to do

Let me ask you something. When you were growing up, did conversations feel safe? Did you get to say what you actually thought and felt without punishment, without shaming, without someone using your words against you later? Or did you learn that speaking honestly meant getting hurt?

For most of us with complex trauma, conversations were never neutral. They were minefields. One wrong word could trigger a parent's rage. One honest feeling could be met with dismissal or mockery. One request for help could be used against us for years. So we learned to rehearse. We learned to anticipate every possible response so we could protect ourselves from the inevitable pain.

This is what Peter Levine calls the "preparatory response" in the nervous system. Your body is trying to predict danger before it arrives. And your mind is trying to control the outcome by controlling every variable. Every word. Every tone. Every possible reply. It's exhausting. But it made sense once.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, would say this part has a job. It's trying to keep you safe. It's trying to prevent you from being blindsided by rejection, criticism, or abandonment. It's trying to make sure you're not caught off guard by the kind of pain you've already survived a hundred times before.

Stay with me here. That part is not your enemy. It's a scared kid who learned that preparation equals survival.

The problem is that this strategy, which might have worked in your childhood home, is now running your adult life. And it's costing you everything.

The cost of constant rehearsal

Think about what happens when you spend hours rehearsing a conversation. You're not present. You're not in your body. You're not actually having the conversation. You're somewhere in the future, fighting a battle that hasn't started yet. And when the real conversation finally happens, you're so drained from the rehearsal that you can barely speak. Or you're so locked into your script that you can't actually hear what the other person is saying.

I've done this so many times. I'd spend three days preparing to tell my partner something difficult. I'd practice every possible response. I'd have counter-arguments ready. I'd have escape routes planned. And then when I finally said the thing, she'd respond with something completely unexpected. Something gentle. Something kind. And I'd be lost. Because my nervous system was still braced for the attack that never came.

Wild, right? You're preparing for a war that doesn't exist anymore.

Janina Fisher talks about this in her work on structural dissociation. The part that's rehearsing is often a "fight" or "freeze" response that got stuck in time. It's still operating from the assumption that you're powerless, that you have no control, that you need to outthink everyone around you just to survive. But you're not that kid anymore. You're an adult with choices. You can walk away from conversations. You can set boundaries. You can say "I need a minute." You can change your mind.

The rehearsing part doesn't know this yet. It's still running the old program.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Fisher's work is central to this understanding.

What happens when you try to stop rehearsing

So you've realized this part is running the show. You've read the books. You've done the therapy. You know you're supposed to "stop overthinking" and "just be present." And you try. You really try. You walk into a conversation without a script. You try to just let the words come naturally. And what happens?

Panic. Pure, unfiltered panic. Your heart races. Your throat tightens. Your mind goes blank. You can't find any words at all. Or you blurt out something you immediately regret. Or you dissociate and feel like you're watching yourself from across the room.

This is normal. This is your nervous system saying, "Wait, we're not doing the thing that kept us alive? We're just going to walk in there unprotected? Are you insane?"

You're not insane. You're asking a protector to stand down without giving it a new way to protect you. That never works.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his work at the Trauma Center, showed that the body needs new experiences, not just new thoughts. You can't think your way out of a protective pattern that lives in your nervous system. You have to show your body, over and over, that you're safe now. That you can handle whatever happens. That you don't need to rehearse anymore because you have other resources.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why you learned to rehearse in the first place.

How to work with the rehearsing part instead of fighting it

Here's what I've learned. You can't fire this part. You can't exile it. You can't shame it into silence. But you can build a different relationship with it.

Start by noticing the rehearsal as it's happening. Don't judge it. Just notice. "Oh, there's that part again. It's really working hard to keep me safe. It must be scared about something." That's it. That's the first step.

Then, try something counterintuitive. Thank it. Out loud or in your head. "Thank you for trying to protect me. I know you're working so hard. I appreciate you." You might feel ridiculous. You might feel like you're talking to a child. You are. Because that part is a child. A very scared, very young part of you that learned this strategy before you had words for what was happening.

Then, make a deal with it. "I'm going to have this conversation. But I'm going to check in with you afterward. If anything goes wrong, I'll listen to you. I'll take your concerns seriously. But right now, I need to try something different."

This is the negotiation that Internal Family Systems teaches. You're not dismissing the protector. You're acknowledging its value while also asserting your adult capacity to handle the situation.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up? Did you feel relief? Did you feel resistance? That's the part responding. Stay curious about it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has specific exercises for working with protective parts like this one.

The conversation you're actually afraid of

I want to be honest with you about something. The rehearsing isn't really about the conversation. It's about what the conversation represents. It's about the possibility of being rejected. Of being seen as too much. Of being abandoned. Of being wrong. Of being bad.

That's the real fear. And the rehearsing part is trying to prevent that fear from becoming real. But here's the thing. It can't. No amount of preparation can guarantee that someone won't reject you. No script can ensure that you won't be misunderstood. No amount of rehearsal can make you safe from being human.

I know that's not comforting. Honestly, it's not supposed to be. It's the truth. And the truth, when you've been living in rehearsed safety, feels like falling.

But here's what I've found on the other side of that fall. Real conversations are messy. They're awkward. They're imperfect. They're full of pauses and stumbles and words that come out wrong. And they're also where actual connection lives. The rehearsed conversation is safe but hollow. The real conversation is risky but alive.

Your part doesn't believe that yet. It might never fully believe it. But you can start showing it, one conversation at a time, that you can survive the mess. That you can handle the awkward silence. That you can say "I didn't say that right, let me try again" and the world doesn't end.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It addresses the root of why you learned to overprepare.

What to do when the rehearsal starts

It's going to start again. Maybe tonight. Maybe five minutes from now. When it does, try this.

Put your hand on your chest. Take a breath. Say to that part, "I see you. I hear you. I know you're scared. I'm going to handle this. You can rest now."

And then do something that grounds you in the present moment. Feel your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name three things you can see. Your nervous system needs to know that you're here, now, not in the future conversation that hasn't happened yet.

Deb Dana, who writes about polyvagal theory, calls this "glimmering" - finding small cues of safety in your environment. It's not about stopping the rehearsing. It's about giving your system an alternative. A place to land.

You don't have to do this perfectly. You don't have to stop rehearsing forever. You just have to start noticing. And then, maybe, start trusting that you can handle what comes.

You've already survived so much. You can survive an imperfect conversation. You can survive someone's disappointment. You can survive being misunderstood. You've done it before. That part just forgot.

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
